explanation_md: After `std::cin >> n` reads `42`, the `'\n'` remains in the buffer. `std::getline` immediately consumes that newline and returns an empty string. So `line` is `""` and the output is `42|` (with nothing after the pipe). The `Hello World` line is never read.

prompt_md: You need to read a full name that may contain spaces (e.g. `Jane Doe`). Which approach is correct?

options.a: `std::cin >> name;` — it reads everything until newline.
options.b: `std::getline(std::cin, name);` — it reads until the newline, including spaces.

explanation_md: `>>` on a `std::string` stops at the first whitespace, so it reads only `Jane`. `std::getline` reads until the newline and includes spaces, giving `Jane Doe`. Option c is for `char` arrays. Option d would put `Jane` in `name` and `Doe` in `rest` — closer but not a single variable.
`>>` skips whitespace between tokens. `a` gets `"cat"` and `b` gets `"dog"`. The output reverses them: `dog cat`.

prompt_md: After `std::cin >> n` (where n is an int), you call `std::cin.ignore()`. What does `ignore()` discard by default?

options.a: All remaining characters in the buffer up to and including the next newline.
options.b: Exactly one character from the buffer.
options.c: All whitespace characters until the next non-whitespace character.

explanation_md: Called with no arguments, `std::cin.ignore()` discards exactly one character. To discard everything through the next newline you must pass a count and delimiter: `std::cin.ignore(std::numeric_limits<std::streamsize>::max(), '\n')`. The no-argument form is often sufficient when you know exactly one `'\n'` follows the previous read.

options.a: `std::getline` does not work after `>>`; you must use `std::cin.get()` instead.
options.b: The `'\n'` left in the buffer by `>>` is immediately consumed by `std::getline`, returning an empty string.
options.c: `std::cin` and `std::getline` cannot share the same stream object.
options.d: `std::string` cannot be passed to `std::getline` — it requires a `char` array.

explanation_md: `>>` leaves the newline in the buffer. `std::getline` reads up to and discards the next `'\n'`, which it finds immediately, so `sentence` is empty. The fix is to call `std::cin.ignore()` (or the max-limit version) between the `>>` and the `std::getline`.
